text: ANNOT, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Lower Alps, and arrond. of Castellane.—The cant. comprises 8 communes, and in 1831 contained 4,688 inhabitants. The town is situated on the l. bank of the Vaire, 12 m. NE of Castellane, and 24 m. ESE of Digne. It has considerable leather manufactories. Pop. 1,178.
ANNOTO. See ANOTTA.
ANNOUNAH, a town of Algiers, in the prov. of Constantina, 32 m. ESE of the town of that name. In its environs are numerous remains of antiquity.
ANNOVER. See HANOVER.
ANNOVER. See ANOVER.
ANNOVILLE, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of La Manche, and cant. of Montmartinsur-Mer, on a small bay of the W coast, 8 m. NW of Coutances. Pop. 993.

ANN'S (Sr.), a small port on the E side of Cape Breton, in N lat. 47°.—Also a headland on the coast of Pembrokeshire, at the entrance of Milford haven, in N lat. 51° 40′ 59", W long. 5° 10′ 2". There are two lighthouses on this point.—Also a port on the W coast of the island of Curação, on the coast of Colombia.
ANN'S (BAY OF ST.), a small gulf on the coast of Africa, 5 leagues N by E from Cape Coroveiro.— Also a town of Jamaica, 12 m. W of Dry harbour.
ANNUNCIADA (Point), a cape of Western Africa, on the coast of Guinea, N of Cape Negro, in S lat. 15° 30'. The coast near this begins to exhibit high cliffs which extend 6 or 7 leagues to the SW.
ANNUNCIATION STRAIT, the channel between the Russian islands of New Siberia and Fadeeffskoi, in the Arctic sea, in N lat. 75°, E long. 146°.
ANNWEILER, a town of Bavaria, circle of Pfalz, on the Queich river, 10 m. W of Landau, in N lat. 49° 12′, E long. 7° 57′. Pop. 2,602. It is a place of active manufacturing industry.
ANOBES, a town of Spain, in Catalonia, prov. of Lerida, about 50 m. NE of Lerida.
ANOETA, a town of Spain, in Guipuscoa, prov. of St. Sebastian, near the r. bank of the Oria, 15 m. SSW of St. Sebastian.
ANOPSHEHR, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Agra, 68 m. ESE of Delhi. This place was ceded to the British in 1801.
ANOR, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Nord, cant. of Trelon, 12 m. SE of Avesnes. Pop. 2,552. There are extensive iron-works here.
ANOST, a commune of France, dep. of Saone-et- Loire, cant. of Lucenay. Pop. 3,000.
ANOULD, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Vosges, cant. of Fraize. Pop. 2,234.
ANOURAMA, a river of Brazil, prov. of Para. ዾኯኯዾኯኯኯኯኯ፟ዾ፟ቜፙ running E, and falling into the Maranon between the Urupi and Maracupuca.
ANOWKIANG, or KINDOWA, a river in the Birman empire, prov. of Pegu, forming one of the branches by which the Irawaddi falls into the bay of Bengal.
ANOZA, a town of Spain, prov. of Palencia, 27 m. NW of Palencia, 14 m. SW of Carrion.
ANRADT, a village of Prussia, in the gov. of Dusseldorf, 6 m. SSW of Creveld. Pop. 840.
ANROCHTE, a village of Prussia, in the circle and 8 m. SSW of Lippstadt. Pop. 900.
ANS, a commune and village of Belgium, cant. of Liege, nearly 2 m. WNW of Liege, on the Waremme railway. Pop. 3,852.
ANS (RAS EL), a cape on the Arabian gulf, forming the northern boundary of Foul bay, in N lat. 23° 54', E long. 35° 44'.
ANSASCA. See ANZA.
ANSAUVILLERS-EN-CHAUSE'E, a commune and village of France, dep. of Oise, 6 m. SE of Breteuil, and 18 ENE of Beauvais. Pop. 1,179. Linens, stockings, and hats, are manufactured here.
ANSBACH. See ANSPACH.

ANSE, a commune and town of France, dep. of the Rhône, 4 m. S of Villefranche, near the Saône, in a beautiful level country. Pop. 1,600. It is celebrated in ecclesiastical history on account of the councils held at it.—Also a canton in the same dep., comprising 14 com., with a pop. in 1831 of 8,580.
ANSEDONIA, a village of Tuscany, prov. of Sienna, 6 m. E of Orbitello, situated on the coast, on the site of the ancient Cosa Volcientium.
ANSEGHEM, a commune and town of Belgium, in West Flanders, arrond. of Courtrai. Pop. 4,397.
ANSEMBURG, a village of Luxemburg, 6 m. NW of Luxemburg, on the Eischen. Iron-works exist here.
ANSEREMME, a commune of Belgium, in the arrond. and 13 m. SW of Dinant, on the Meuse. Pop. 407.

ANSES A' PITRE, a river of Hayti, rising in the N part of the Bahoruco mountains, running NNW, and falling into the sea opposite Cocqueviel shoal. It is not navigable; but large vessels may anchor at about 1 m. off its mouth.
ANSES-D'ARLET (LES), a commune and town of Martinique, 7 m. S of Fort-Royal. The coffee produced in this district is highly esteemed.
ANSGA, a town of Asiatic Turkey, prov. of Algezira, pash. of Diarbekir, 36 m. NE of Malatia, on a river which runs into the Euphrates.
ANSLEY, a parish in Warwickshire, 5 m. W by N of Nuneaton. Area 3,580 acres. Pop. 701.
ANSLOW, or Annesley, a township in Rolleston parish, Staffordshire. Area 1,920 acres. Pop. 278.

ANSON, a co. of North Carolina, U.S., bordering on South Carolina, and bounded on the E by the Yadkin river. Area 760 sq. m. Pop. 15,077, of whom 5,300 were slaves. The chief town is Wadesboro'.— Also a town of Maine, U.S., in the co. of Somerset, on the W side of the Kennebec river. Pop. 1,941.

ANSON'S ISLAND (LORD), or, as named by the natives, Buka, an island in the South Pacific, to the S of Bougainville's island, from which it is separated by a narrow strait. Its northernmost point is in S lat. 5°, E long. 154° 34'. It rises with bold and precipitous cliffs from the surface of the water, and is wooded from the shore to the centre.

ANSOUIS, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Vaucluse, cant. of Pertuis. Pop. 996. ANSPACH, ANSBACH, or ONOLZBACH, a district of Bavaria, formerly an independent territory under the appellation of the margravate of Anspach, and governed by a sovereign prince, with a territory of Turkey, at the base of Mount Ida, on the gulf and. 1,800 sq. m., and a pop. of 250,000. In 1791, the 9 m. W of Adramyt. It has a safe harbour. ANT last margrave, Charles Frederic, retired to England, and ceded his marquisate or principality to Frederic William II., of Prussia. Frederic William ceded A., in 1806, to France; and she exchanged it with Bavaria for Juliers and Berg. Prussia gave up Baireuth also, the other portion of the margravate, in 1807, at the peace of Tilsit, to France; and France transferred it to Bavaria. Bavaria at first distributed the prov. into the circles of Rezat, Altmuhl, and Pegnitz; but in 1810, Anspach was included in the circles of the Rezat and the Upper Danube.—The town of An- SPACH is situated in a rich valley on the Rezat, 30 m. SW of Nuremberg, in N lat. 48° 12', E long. 10° 33'. It contains a pop. of 11,420, of whom about 600 are Catholics. It is the seat of a Protestant consistory, and has a gymnasium, and a public library of 17,000 volumes. Its manufactures are numerous and flourishing; consisting of woollen, linen, cotton, and silkstuffs, porcelain, playing-cards, tobacco, white lead, and leather.—Also a village of Germany, in Nassau, 17 m. SSW of Usingen. Pop. 1,200.

ANSTEY, a parish in Hertfordshire, 3 m. SE of Barkway. Area 2,170 acres. Pop. 497.—Also a chapelry in Thurcaston p., Leicestershire. Area 1,400 acres. Pop. 838.—Also a p. in the county of the city of Coventry, in Warwickshire, 5 m. NE of Coventry. Area 990 acres. Pop. 224.—Also a p. in Wiltshire, 5 m. SE of Hindon. Area 840 acres. Pop. 329.
ANSTEY (East and West), two parishes in the hund. of South Molton, Devonshire, 3 m. WSW of Dulverton. Area of E A. 2,170 acres. Pop. 240. Area of W A. 2,820 acres. Pop. 279.

ANSTON, a parish in the W. R. of Yorkshire, 6 m. W of Worksop, comprising the township of Woodsetts, with an area of 1,380 acres, and a pop. of 181; and the township of North and South Anston, with an area of 3,110 acres, and a pop. of 921.

ANSTRUTHER-EASTER, a royal borough, seaport, and parish of Fife, situated on the frith of Forth, 4 m. W of Crail. Pop. in 1831, 1,007; in 1841, 997. The harbour is the best on this coast below Burntisland. It has an area of 6 acres enclosed by piers. The arrivals in 1846 were 202 vessels of 9,715 tons. Corn is the principal export: the imports are chiefly from the Baltic and Germany. The burgh, in conjunction with Anstruther - Wester, Crail, Kilrenny, and Pittenweem, sends one member to parliament.
ANSTRUTHER-WESTER, a royal burgh and parish in the shire of Fife, at the mouth of a rivulet which separates it from Anstruther-Easter. Pop. of the p. in 1841, 449, of whom 339 were in the burgh.

ANTA, a town of Peru, prov. of Cuzco, on a lake which discharges its waters into the Quillabamba river, 30 m. W of Cuzco.—Also arriver of Brazil, prov. of Rio Grande, formed by several streams which rise in the Sierra de Tapolfama de Montegrande. It joins the Tibicoari river, after a course of upwards of 100 m. from E to W.
ANTAGICHA, a town of Turkey, in the Herzegovine, 70 m. NW of Mostar.
ANTAKIA. See ANTIOCH.
ANTAKOSKI, a town of Russia, in the gov. of Archangel, on the Tana river, in N lat. 69° 30'.

ANTALO, a town of Abyssinia, in Tigrè, the cap. of Enderta, and residence of the governor of the province, 75 m. SSE of Axum. It is a considerable place, and contains about 1,000 houses. The plain of A. stretches above 80 m. from E to W. and probably has an elevation of 8,000 ft. Around the town of A. it is very fertile; but it becomes wild and uncultivated towards the W.
ANTANARIVO. See TANANARIVO.
ANTANDROS, or ANTANDRO, a town of Asiatic

ANTAO (SAO), a town of Brazil, prov. of Pernambuco, 40 m. W of Pernambuco, on the l. bank of the Tapacora.—Also one of the Cape Verd islands. See St. Anthony. - Also a town in Portuguese Africa, the cap. of Prince's island. Pop. 1,000.

ANTARCTIC OCEAN, the expanse of water surrounding the South or Antarctic pole. It may be strictly regarded as extending from the pole to the Antarctic circle, or 66° 30' S, where the sea receives the appellation of the Great Southern ocean; but the word is often used to express generally the oceanic regions round the South pole, without regard to the limits of the Antarctic circle. This portion of the globe's surface has been hitherto very imperfectly explored, and is even less familiar to navigators than corresponding latitudes in the opposite polar region. It is ascertained, however, to be much less habitable than the Arctic regions, since it is occupied either with sea or ice in latitudes corresponding to parts of the northern hemisphere far within the limits of man's occupation. The earliest navigators who are known to have passed the Antarctic circle, or the parallel of 66° 30' S, are Cook and Furneaux. On the 17th of January, 1773, Cook reached S lat. 67° 15', E long. 39°, where he found the ice firm and unbroken as far as the eye could reach. On the 30th of January, 1774, he reached S lat. 71° 10', W long. 106° 54', when he was again forced to return. In 1819, the Russian navigator Bellinghausen reached S lat. 70°; and in 1821 he discovered Alexander's and Peter's lands, or islands rather, the former in the parallel of 68° 43', the latter in 68° 57', being the most southern land known at the time. On the 20th of February, 1823, Captain Weddell reached Slat. 74° 15', W long. 34° 16' 45", in an open sea clear of field ice, and having "many whales in sight, and innumerable birds:" but he was obliged to forego any further examination by the prevalence of a S wind, and the necessity of working his way back through 1,000 miles of sea interspersed with ice-islands, at an advanced season of the year; for though in that high latitude, no field ice, and only four ice-islands were visible, and in 72° 38' "not a particle of ice of any description was to be seen," yet in 68° and 69° iceislands were so numerous as almost to prevent progress. The mere latitude is, therefore, no sure criterion of the possibility of navigation. On the 7th of January, 1839, the schooner Eliza Scott of 154 tons, commanded by Captain Balleny, with her consort the cutter Sabrina of 54 tons, sailed from the S end of New Zealand for seal-fishing purposes. On the 24th they crossed Bellinghausen's track, and on the 1st of February had reached S lat. 69°, in E long. 172°, or 220 m. to the S of Bellinghausen's extreme point. Working to the NW they discovered a group of islands, five in number, in S lat. 66°, and E long 163°, the summit of one of which, emitting smoke, was estimated at 12,000 ft. On the 2d of March, in S lat. 69° 58', E long. 121° 8', they discovered Sabrina land. In 1840, a French expedition under Captain Dumont D'Urville sailed from Hobart town. They discovered land on the evening of the 19th, and traced it in a continuous line in about the latitude of the Antarctic circle, and between the long. of 136° and 142° E. This land was without the least appearance of vegetation; and its general height was estimated at 1,300 ft. M. D'Urville named it Terre Adélie. Proceeding westwards they sailed about 60 m. along a solid wall of ice, 150 ft. high, which was named Cote Clairée, and which they traced till the western part turned away suddenly to the S. Nearly simultaneously with the French expedition, an American exploring expedition, under Captain Wilkes, sailed into these seas, but did not get beyond the parallel of 67°. The furthest point in these latitudes hitherto attained, viz., 78° 10', was reached by Captain James Ross in 1842. This last mentioned navigator has succeeded in establishing, with tolerable certainty, the existence of an Antarctic continent. In a despatch to the secretary of the Admiralty he says that after passing among many icebergs to the southward of 63°, they made the pack edge, and entered the Antarctic circle on the 1st day of January, 1841. "This pack," he continues, "presented none of those formidable characters which I had been led to expect from the accounts of the Americans and French; but the circumstances were sufficiently unfavourable to deter me from entering it at this time, and a gale from the northward interrupted our operations for three or four days. On the 5th of January we again made the pack, about 100 m. to the eastward, in lat. 66° 45' S, and long. 174° 16' E; and although the wind was blowing directly on it, with a high sea running, we succeeded in entering it without either of the ships sustaining any injury, and, after penetrating a few miles, we were enabled to make our way to the southward with comparative ease and safety. On the following three or four days our progress was rendered more difficult and tedious by thick fogs, light winds, a heavy swell, and almost constant snow-showers; but a strong water-sky to the SE, which was seen at every interval of clear weather, encouraged us to persevere in that direction; and on the morning of the 9th, after sailing more than 200 m. through this pack, we gained a perfectly clear sea, and bore away SW towards the magnetic pole. On the morning of the 11th of January, when in lat. 70° 41' S, and long. 172° 36' E, land was discovered at the distance, as it afterwards proved, of nearly 100 m., directly in the course we were steering, and therefore directly between us and the Pole. Although this circumstance was viewed at the time with considerable regret, as being likely to defeat one of the more important objects of the expedition, yet it restored to ANT England the honour of the discovery of the southernmost known land, which had been nobly won, and for more than twenty years possessed, by Russia. Continuing our course towards this land for many hours, we seemed scarcely to approach it. It rose in lofty mountain peaks of from 9,000 to 12,000 ft. in height, perfectly covered with eternal snow; the glaciers that descended from near the mountain-summits projected many miles into the ocean, and presented a perpendicular face of lofty cliffs. As we neared the land, some exposed patches of rock appeared, and steering towards a small bay for the purpose of effecting a landing, we found the shore so thickly lined for some miles with bergs and pack-ice, and with a heavy swell dashing against it, that we were obliged to abandon our purpose and steer towards a more promising looking point to the SE, off which we observed several small islands; and on the morning of the 12th I landed, accompanied by Commander Crozier and a number of the officers of each ship, and took possession of the country in the name of her most gracious Majesty Queen Victoria. The island on which we landed is composed wholly of igneous rocks, numerous specimens of which, with other imbedded minerals, were procured. It is in lat. 71° 56' S, and long. 171° 7' E. Observing that the E coast of the mainland tended to the southward, whilst the north shore took a NW direction, I was led to hope that, by penetrating to the south as far as practicable, it might be possible to pass beyond the magnetic pole, which our combined observations placed in 76° S nearly; and thence, by steering westward, complete its circumnavigation. We accordingly pursued our course along this magnificent land; and on the 22d of January we reached 74° 15' S, the highest southern latitude that had ever been attained by any preceding navigator, and that by our own countryman, Captain James Weddell. "Although greatly impeded by strong southerly gales, thick fogs, and constant snow-storms, we continued the examination of the coast to the southward, and on the 27th we again landed on an island in lat. 76° 8' S, long. 168° 12' E, composed, as on the former occasion, entirely of igneous rocks. Still steering to the southward, early the next morning, the 28th, a mountain of 12,400 ft. above the level of the sea was seen emitting flame and smoke in splendid profusion. This magnificent volcano received the name of Mount Erebus. It is in lat. 77° 32' S, and long. 167° E; an extinct crater to the eastward of Mount Erebus, of a somewhat less elevation, was called Mount Terror. The mainland preserved its southerly tending, and we continued to follow it until, in the afternoon, when close in with the land, our further progress in that direction was prevented by a barrier of ice stretching away from a projecting cape of the coast directly to the ESE. This extraordinary barrier presented a perpendicular face of at least 150 ft., rising, of course, far above the mast-heads of our ships, and completely concealing from our view every thing beyond it, except only the tops of a range of very lofty mountains in a SSE direction, and in latitude 79° S. Pursuing the examination of this splendid barrier to the eastward, we reached the lat. of 78° 4' S, the highest we were at any time able to attain, on the 2d of February; and on the 9th, having traced its continuity to the longitude of 191° 23', in lat. 78° S, a distance of more than 300 m., our further progress was prevented by a heavy pack pressed closely against the barrier; and the narrow lane of water by means of which we had penetrated thus far became so completely covered by rapidly forming ice that nothing but the strong breeze with which we were favoured enabled us to retrace our steps. When at a distance of less than half-a-mile from its lofty icy cliffs, we had soundings with 318 fathoms, on a bed of soft blue mud. With a temperature of 20° below the freezing point we found the ice to form so rapidly on the surface, that any further examination of the barrier in so extremely severe a period of the season being impracticable, we stood away to the westward, for the purpose of making another attempt to approach the magnetic pole, and again reached its lat. (76° S) on the 15th of February; and although we found that much of the heavy ice had drifted away since our former attempt, and its place in a great measure supplied by recently formed ice, yet we made some way through it, and got a few miles nearer the pole than we had before been able to accomplish, when the heavy pack again frustrated all our efforts, completely filling the space of 15 or 16 m. between us and the shore. We were this time in lat. 76° 12' S, and long. 164°; the dip being 88'40, and variation 109'24 E. We were, of course, only 160 m. from the pole. Had it been possible to approach any part of this coast, and have found any place of security for the ships, we might have travelled this short distance over the land; but this proved to be utterly impracticable. And although our hopes of complete attainment have not been realized, it is some satisfaction to feel assured that we have approached the pole more nearly, by some hundred miles than any of our predecessors; and from the multitude of observations that have been made in both ships, and in so many different directions from it, its position can be determined with nearly as much accuracy as if we had actually reached the spot itself. It had ever been an object of anxious desire with us to find a harbour for the ships, so as to enable us to make simultaneous observations with the numerous observatories that would be at work on the important term-day of the 28th of February, as well as for other scientific purposes; but every part of the coast where indentations appeared, and where harbours on other shores usually occur, we found so perfectly filled with perennial ice of many hundred feet in thickness, that all our endeavours to find a place of shelter for our vessels were quite unavailing." "Having now completed all that it appeared to me possible to accomplish in so high a latitude, and at so advanced a period of the season, and desirous to obtain as much information as possible of the extent and form of the coast we had discovered, as also to guide in some measure our future operations, I bore away, on the 18th of February, for the N part of this land, [Van Diemen's Land,] and which, by favour of a strong southerly gale, we reached on the morning of the 21st. We again endeavoured to effect a landing on this part of the coast, and were again defeated in our attempt by the heavy pack, which extended for many miles from the shore, and rendered it impossible. For several days we continued to examine the coast to the westward, tracing the packedge along until, on the 25th of February, we found the land abruptly to terminate in lat. 70° 40' S, and long. 165° E, tending considerably to the S of W, and presenting to our view an immense space occupied by a dense pack, now so firmly cemented together by the newly formed ice, and so covered by recent snow, as to present the appearance of one unbroken mass, and defying every attempt to penetrate it. The great southern land we have discovered, whose continuity we have traced from nearly the 70th to the 79th deg. of latitude, I am desirous to distinguish by the name of our most gracious Majesty Queen Victoria. Following the edge of the pack to the NW, as weather permitted, we found it to occupy the whole space between the NW shore of the great southern land and the chain of islands lying near the Antarctic circle, first discovered by Balleny in 1839, and more extensively explored by the American and French expeditions in the following year." A second exploring voyage within the Antarctic regions was commenced by Captain, now Sir James Ross, on the 15th of November, 1841. The ships entered the ice pack on the 18th of December, in S lat. 62°. Here they were entangled until February 2d, 1842, making no more progress during that time than to 68°, where they emerged into comparatively open water to the southward of a large body of the pack, which, however, trended W. At this time the season was far advanced, and as, in the preceding year, the retreat had been commenced through absolute necessity on the 9th of February, so Captain Ross did not think proper now to re-enter the pack-ice, but proceeded along its edge to the westward, advancing as far as 187° W, and then to the southward and eastward. On the 23d of February the expedition came in view of the grand Victoria Barrier. The day being fine, the voyagers approached within 11 m. of the Barrier, finally reaching lat. 78° 10′ S, in W long. 162°, having made six miles farther than in the preceding year, the highest latitude hitherto attained. Unable to proceed eastward, the retreat was commenced along the pack edge. Seaweed was met with on reaching the parallel of 64°, and occasionally seen when running down the parallel of 60°, from 170° to 80° W, and thence in great abundance to the Falkland islands, where the ships anchored in Berkeley sound on the 6th of April, 1842, not having seen land for 138 days, since leaving New Zealand. The third cruise to the South Polar regions was commenced on the morning of the 17th of December, 1842, when the expedition sailed from Berkeley sound. An opportunity was afforded again of tracing the southern limit of sea-weeds. The Macrocystis was lost in S lat. 55°, W long. 57°; but on attaining lat. 63°, long. 54°, another species, the Scytothalia Jacquinotii appeared, which had been originally discovered by Webster during the stay of the Chanticleer at Deception island, one of the South Shetland group. On the 28th, land was made, being a portion of Palmer's Land, to which the name of Terre Louis Philippe has since been given by D'Urville. The ships were already in the pack-ice, through which they penetrated, tracing the land to 64° S. Unable, after a series of fruitless efforts, to penetrate farther than 65°, and after having been more or less entangled in the ice for thirty-seven days, Sir James Ross finally bore up, and when, with great difficulty, the ships had been extricated from the pack-ice, commenced tracing its edge to the eastward. A succession of easterly gales rendered the progress in the advancing season tedious and hazardous. At last, however, on the 22d of February, 1843, the pack was lost sight of, trending to the SW. On the 28th, the Antarctic circle was recrossed; and in spite of the rapidly shortening days, dark nights, and continual bad weather-for throughout the month of February, corresponding to an English August, only one day elapsed without snow—the commander persevered in holding a southerly course. On Sunday, the 5th of March, the weather being thick, with snow-squalls, white petrels were seen, a bird whose appearance affords a sure indication of the proximity of pack-ice, and on the afternoon of the same day a heavy pack was descried, only a few yards ahead, with a terrific surf beating on it. The ice here was such as not to allow of being "taken" (or entered), even under the most favourable circumstances, and the ships were accordingly put about in lat. 71° 30' S, W long. 15°. The thickness of the weather made it impossible to ascertain the course and position of the pack, and the northward voyage was commenced under violent NE equinoctial gales. Beating to the northward, the ice occurred on both tacks, and the vessels were found to be in a bight of the pack, with the ocean loaded with bergs, and while the continued snow-squalls prevented the possibility of seeing any object ahead, the heavy seas and snow-laden state of the rigging rendered all human exertions ineffectual. From that date to the 11th of March, matters remained much the same, the ships beating to the northward with as much press of sail as could be exposed, trusting to Providence alone for guidance among the bergs. On the 19th, the position assigned to Bouvet's or Circumcision island was gained; but the weather ANT rendered all endeavours, for three days, to discover land in this place of no avail. Both ships had a narrow escape of running foul of an iceberg, over which the sea was breaking eighty feet high. The last berg was seen on March 25th, in 47° S, the ships finally gaining the cape of Good Hope on the 4th of April, 1843, within two or three years after they had first quitted that port for the high southern latitudes. The absolute limits of vegetable life have not yet been attained in the far regions of the North; but not the minutest trace of a moss or an algæ was discovered on the vast Antarctic continent traced by Sir James Ross; nor is it possible that any tribes of the human family can exist in these high southern latitudes; but the animal kingdom has numerous representatives here. The blue petrel and the penguin are everywhere to be seen; and the hump-backed and finned-back whale abound. The sperm whale, the sea-elephant—a species of seal so called from its possessing an elongated snout,-and the sea-leopard-a species of seal so called from its spots-have been observed not far from the Antarctic limits, viz., about the shores of the South Shetland islands, in lat. 61° to 63°. The seals frequenting these parts are exclusively those which yield the fur well-known in the manufacture of caps. These inoffensive animals at first exhibited no alarm at the approach of man; but the frequent repetition of his invasions has either stimulated or educated their instinct so far as to induce them to adopt measures of self-defence or escape. The improvident and eager avarice of adventurers has gone far to thin their numbers, and threatens their speedy extermination; the general ignorance of their habits, combined with the impatient thirst for immediate gain, having led to the destruction of the mothers at a period when the young were entirely dependent on them for continued existence.
ANTARI, a village of Portuguese Africa, on the N bank of the Zambezi, below Boca de Rio. The river above this presents an archipelago of islets amid shallow channels scarcely floating a canoe.
ANTAS, a town of Portugal, prov. of Beira, 18 m. E of Viseu.
ANTCHEOO, a town of Corea, about 18 m. from the mouth of a small river which runs into the Hoang- Hai, or Yellow sea, nearly 160 m. NNW of Kingkitao, in N lat. 39° 31', E long. 126° 3'.

ANTENEGO, a town of Upper California, on the l. bank of an affluent of the Rio Salado, in N lat. 38° 25'. ANTEQUERA, ANTICARIA, or ANTECARIA, a town of Spain, forming with its adjacent territory a separate prov., situated between Granada and Seville, 18 m. NNW of Malaga. Pop. 19,000. There are manufactories of silk and cotton goods, paper, and Marocco leather in the town and environs.
ANTEQUERA (SIERRA DE), a chain of mountains in Spain, prov. of Malaga, running E and W. It is a continuation of the Sierra Nevada; and unites itself to the Sierra Ronda in the province of Cadiz.
ANTERRIEUX, a commune of France, in the dep. of Cantal, cant. of Chaudesaigues. Pop. 339.
ANTES, a township in Huntingdon co., Pennsylvania, U. S., 116 m. from Harrisburg. Pop. 2,154.
ANTHE'E, a commune and village of Belgium, in the prov. of Namur, 12 m. NE of Philippeville. There are iron-works here. Pop. 656.
ANTHEIT, a commune and village of Belgium, in the prov. of Liege, 3 m. N of Huy. Pop. 1,427. Coal is wrought here.

ANTHEME (SAINT), a canton, commune, and town of France, dep. of Puy-de-Dome. The cant., comprising 5 com., had a pop. of 6,874 in 1831. The com. and t., 9 m. E of Ambert, on the Ance, has a pop. of 3,826.
ANTHERSBURG, a town of Upper Canada, on the W coast of the peninsula formed by Lake St. Clair and Lake Erie, in N lat. 41° 50'.
ANTHI, a town of Savoy, nearly 3 m. WSW of Thonon, in N lat. 46° 20'.
ANTHOLZ, a village of the Tyrol, in the Pusterthal, 4 m. ENE of Prunecken, celebrated for its mineral baths.

ANTHONY (CAPE St.), a cape in Arabia, on the S coast of Yemen, in the sea of Bab-el-Mandeb, between Wadi Meidam and the straits of Bab-el-Mandeb, in N lat. 12° 40', E long. 44° 10'. -Also a cape on the coast of Buenos Ayres, forming the south point of the entrance into the Rio de la Plata, in S lat. 36° 19′ 30″, W long. 56° 32′ 30″. -Also the western extremity of the island of Cuba, in N lat. 21° 54', W long. 84° 56'.—Also a cape of Brazil, on the coast of Todos Santos, in S lat. 13°, W long. 38° 37'.—Also a cape on the coast of the straits of Magalhaen, between the bays of Arenas and Santa Catalina.

ANTHONY (FALLS OF St.), a celebrated fall on the Mississippi, 9 m. above the spot where the St. Peter's river falls into it; 1,029 m. from Lake La Biche or Itasca, the source of the Mississippi; and 843 m. above the junction of the Missouri. The Mississippi is 630 yards broad above the fall; immediately below, it is contracted within a channel of 209 yards. An island divides the fall into two parts, the eastern being 230 yards wide, and the western 310. The island itself is 100 yards wide, and stretches both above and below the fall. Father Hennepin—the first European who visited it, and who gave it the name it now bears in honour of St. Anthony of Padua-states its height to be 50 or 60 ft. Carver reduced it to 30 ft. Major Long agrees with Pike that the perpendicular height of the cataract is 16½ ft., besides 58 ft. more of a rapid immediately below; so that, when viewed from a distance, it appears to be much higher than it really is. When the river is high, the appearance of these falls is very magnificent, as the spray then formed reflects all the colours of the rainbow. The surrounding country is beautiful, exhibiting gentle ascents, with eminences covered with verdure. The portage around the falls is 260 rods long; and the whole fall to the foot of the rapids—which extend several miles—may be 100 ft.

ANTHONY (FORT), a settlement on the coast of Guinea, founded by the Portuguese, but now occupied by the Dutch, 2 m. E of the Ancobra river.- Also a military post on the high bluff which rises on the r. bank of the Mississippi, and I. of the St. Peter, at the confluence of these streams. It effectually commands both rivers.

ANTHONY (PORT), a harbour on the S coast of the island of Stalimene, between Cape Stala and Mount St. Andrew, in N lat. 30° 54', E long. 25° 16'. ANTHONY (St.) or Antonio Santo, the most northern of the Cape Verd islands. Lat. of NW point 17° 12' N, long. 25° 10' W. Topazes are found in one of its mountains, and it is said to contain mines of gold and silver. The inhabitants, chiefly Negroes, are about 600 in number.

ANTHONY'S NOSE, a promontory in New York, U.S., 52 m. N of New York, and 6 m. S of West Point; 1,128 ft. in height, above sea-level, and 877 above the river. There are several other mountainpeaks bearing this name in the state of New York.

ANTHROPO'PHAGI, cannibals, or such as feed on human flesh. It is with difficulty we can bring ourselves to believe that such monsters ever existed; and yet there is scarcely any fact more fully established by the concurring testimony both of ancient and modern authors. Fabulous history is full of accounts of anthropophagi. According to some authors, to eat human flesh was a primitive and universal custom. Thus Euhemerus informs us—as the passage has been transmitted by Ennius, quoted by Lactantius Divin. Institut. vol. c. xiii. p. 59.—that Saturn and Ops, and the rest of mankind in their time, were accustomed to feed on human flesh: Saturnum et Opem, cæterosque tum homines humanam carnem solitos esitare. The first step towards civilization is represented to have been the abolition of this barbarous custom; and Orpheus is thought to have had the merit of this reformation. What Horace says concerning him, cannot well be understood, except as relating to this practice:- Cædibus, et victu fædu, deterruit Orpheus. By the poets, the Læstrygons, the Lamiæ, the Sirens, and the Cyclops, are all celebrated as infamous anthropophagi. Circe and Scylla come under the same character as individuals. A horrid account is given by Homer of the fate of Ulysses's companions in the Cyclops' den:- Torn limb from limb, he spreads the horrid feast, And fierce, devours it like a mountain-beast. ANT He sucks the marrow, and the blood he drains, Nor entrails, flesh, nor solid bone remains. Though these accounts are doubtless overcharged, and mixed with much fable, there can be little doubt that they are founded on the manners of the times; for we find the same accounts given by grave historians, and authenticated by all the evidence which the nature of the case will admit. Strabo, I. 7. p. 463, declares that the Scythians were anthropophagi. Pliny affirms the same in more places than one. The Sacæ, the Indi, and the Indo- Scythæ, are represented by Mela as addicted to the same barbarous custom: Scythæ sunt Androphagi et Sacæ. Indorum quidam, proximos, parentesque, priusquam annis et ægritudine in maciem eant, velut hostias cædunt, cæsorumque visceribus epulare fas, et maxime pium est. Several nations are enumerated by Aristotle as indulging in the same practice, such as the Achæans, the Heniochi, and other nations on the borders of Pontus. Herodotus gives a like account of the Scythians and Indians. These testimonies of ancient authors derive credibility from what has been observed in modern times; and from the practices which have been brought to light by voyagers and travellers. In the ancient world, we hear only of some particular nations of cannibals who fed on human flesh. "But in every part of the New world, there were people to whom this custom was familiar. It prevailed in the Southern continent, in several of the islands, and in various districts of North America. Even in those parts, where circumstances with which we are unacquainted had in a great measure abolished this practice, it seems formerly to have been so well known, that it is incorporated into the idiom of their language. Among the Iroquois, the phrase by which they express their resolution against an enemy is, 'Let us go and eat that nation." The Americans, however, generally speaking, never devoured any but their enemies; and when the Spaniards, in the expedition of Narvaez into Florida, in 1528, were reduced to such distress that, to save their own lives, they were obliged to eat such of their companions as happened to die, it appeared so shock ing to the natives, who had been accustomed to devour none but prisoners, that they were filled with horror and indignation. The practice still prevails among some of the tribes in Terra Firma; and Stedman affirms that the Caribs devour the flesh of their prisoners with the avidity of wolves. We transcribe a passage from this author, as it brings into one view several things connected with our present subject. "I should not forget to mention, that the Gango negroes are supposed to be anthropophagi, or cannibals, like the Caribbee Indians, instigated by habitual and implacable revenge. Among the rebels of this tribe, after the taking of Boucore, some pots were found on the fire, with human flesh, which one of the officers had the curiosity to taste, and declared that it was not inferior to some kinds of beef or pork. I have since been assured, by a Mr. Vaugills, an American, that, having travelled a great number of miles inland in Africa, he at last came to a place where human legs, arms, and thighs, hung upon wooden shambles, and were exposed to sale like butcher meat. And Captain John Keen, formerly of the Dolphin, but late of the Naimbana schooner, in the Sierra Leona company's service, positively assured me, that a few years since, when he was on the coast of Africa, in the brig Fame, from Bristol, Mr. Samuel Biggs, owner, trading for wool, iron, and gold-dust, a Capt. Dunningen, with his whole crew, belonging to the Nassau schooner, were cut in pieces, salted, and eaten by the negroes of Great Drewin." [Stedman's Narrative, &c., vol. ii. p. 267.] But all this is surpassed by what is related on good authority respecting the Giagas, a race of cannibals who are said to have everrun a great part of Africa. These monsters, it is said, are descended from the Agows and Gallas who dwell in the southern extremity of Abyssinia, near the sources of the Nile. Impelled by necessity, or a love of plunder, they left their original settlements, and extended their ravages through the heart of Africa, till they were stopped by the Western ocean. They seized on the kingdom of Benguela, lying to the south of Angola; and in this situation they were found by the Romish missionaries, and by our countryman, Andrew Battel, whose adventures may be found in Purchas's 'Pilgrim.' Both he, and the Capuchin Cavazzi, who resided long amongst them, and converted several of them to Christianity, give such an account of their manners as is enough to chill the blood with horror. We shall spare our readers the horrid detail, only observing, that human flesh is one of their principal delicacies, and that they devour it, not from a spirit of revenge, or from want of other food, but as the most agreeable dainty. Some of their commanders, when they went on an expedition, carried numbers of young women along with them, some of whom were slain almost every day, to gratify this unnatural appetite. [Mod. Univers. Hist., vol. xvi. p. 321.] Messrs. Smith and Gullifer, in an expedition up the Essequibo, a few years ago, fell in with a Carib tribe of Anthropophagi who, as a matter of taste, devoured their female captives, whom they kept confined in a large logic or shed, like so many poultry, till required for their horrid banquets. [Geog. Journ., vol. ii., p. 71.] The natives of New Zealand are cannibals; and Mr. Earle has given very direct evidence of their indulging this hideous propensity without even the pretext or the stimulus of revenge. [Residence in New Zealand, pp. 112-122.] Marsden and Raffles inform us, that the Battas, in the island of Sumatra, eat human flesh as a kind of ceremony, to show their detestation of some particular offences. Garcilasso de la Vega says that, in some of the provinces of Peru, the natives devour their own children whom they had begotten on the captives taken in war; and that they served their mothers after the same manner when they were past child-bearing. [Hist. des Yncas de Peru, 1. i. c. 12.] All the Spanish historians who write concerning the conquest of Mexico agree in stating, that the Mexicans, and all the neighbouring nations of Indians devoured the flesh of their enemies; and B. Diaz, who was an eye-witness of what he relates—and who is considered by Dr. Robertson as most worthy of credit of all the Spanish writers on American affairs—informs us that the Indians who assisted Cortes in the reduction of Mexico, carried off with them, on their return to their own country, large quantities of the flesh of the Mexicans, salted or dried, as a most acceptable present to their friends and relations. [Robertson's America, vol. ii. note 94.] This enumeration of cannibal nations might be greatly extended; but we turn from the disgusting subject, to offer some observations on the origin of this inhuman custom. Three distinct causes may be assigned as giving rise to this practice, -necessity, revenge, or superstition. The first of these causes has operated in a thousand instances; men have often been forced from the extremity of hunger to feed on the flesh of their own species. But no sooner has the dire necessity which impelled to the loathsome act passed away than the practice has been instantly abandoned. Amongst barbarous nations, however, threatened with famine, the practice may have met with a more welcome reception; and feelings of rage superadded to those of hunger may have impelled them to seek a bloody repast from the flesh of their enemies. This, however, is little more than conconjecture; and there are, perhaps, more plausible arguments in support of the opinion that this monstrous custom has been dictated by the fell spirit of revenge. It has most frequently been observed to be practised under the influence of this passion. It is generally the flesh of an enemy that is eaten; and the feasts of human flesh are, for the most part, the concluding scene of a barbarous triumph over a fallen foe. These considerations induced Dr. Robertson to say, that "the rancour of revenge first prompted men to this barbarous action." There are several reasons which prevent us from yielding an unqualified assent to this opinion, and which incline us to think that superstition has at least had its share in driving the passions of men to such barbarous excesses. The passage quoted above, respecting the Indians, in which it is said that some of them reckon it a mark of piety to eat the flesh of their nearest relations, sufficiently demonstrates that the practice has sometimes originated in superstition. The accounts also respecting the Giagas of Africa seem to prove that it is amongst them an established custom altogether independent of the spirit of revenge. Porphyry informs us, that in Chios and Tenedos, the votaries of Dionysius or Bacchus, sacrificed to him, and few or diagrants, tearing a man limb from limb, and eating him no doubt; for the wuoquyia, or eating of raw flesh was one of the peculiar rites in the Dionysiac mysteries. Is it not probable then, that, in many instances, the custom of which we are speaking had its origin in superstition,—a principle which has always been found more capable of driving men to horrible extremes, than the most violent of the natural passions? The same principle which led men to believe that the gods could be propitiated by the blood of captive victims, would naturally lead them to imagine that the sacrifice would be more acceptable, when accompanied with the deepest circumstances of horror.

ANTIBES, a town of France, dep. of the Var, arrond. of Grasse, 12 m. ESE of Grasse, 24 m. ENE of Frejus, near the Sardinian frontier; in N lat. 43° 35'9", E long. 7° 7' 30" [Côtes de France, 1845]. Pop. 6,080. It is built on the E side of a small peninsula which divides the gulf of Juan from the estuary of the Var. This is a military place of the third class; and, as a barrier on the side of Italy, is of great importance in a military point of view. Its port, 1,012 ft. long by 270 ft. wide, is protected by a mole, but has been so choked with sand that it can only receive vessels of small burden. The citadel is built on a rock near the sea. The commerce of A. includes salt fish, oranges, citrons, figs, raisins, oil, wine, and pottery-ware. It is a very ancient city, having been founded, under the name of Antipolis, by a Greek colony from Marseilles in 340 B. C. It was made a military station by the Romans. After the fall of the Empire, it suffered under the invasions of the barbarians, and was subsequently destroyed by the Saracens. Francis I. and Henry IV. fortified it. In 1746 it successfully withstood the attacks of the English, Imperialists, and Piedmontese. In 1815 it was the only place of which the garrison refused to join Napoleon on his return from Elba.
ANTICO (SANTO), an island off the W coast of Sardinia, in N lat. 39° 5', forming the western side of the gulf of Palmas. 1,120.
ANTICOLE, or ANTICOLI, a town of the Papal states, in the prov. and 11 m. N of Frosinone, in N lat. 41° 49′, E long. 13° 14′. Pop. 1,000. ANT
ANTICOLI CORRADO, a town of the Papal states, in the district of Rome, 10 m. ENE of Tivoli. Pop. 1,000.

ANTICOSTI, an island in the gulf of St. Lawrence, lying WSW and ESE in the widest part of its estuary, between the meridians of 61° 45' and 64° 15' W, and dividing the entrance of the river into two channels from 20 to 40 m. in width. It is about 125 m. in length; its extreme breadth is 30 m.; its superficial area 1,530,000 acres. Its surface is in general low, and covered with forests of stunted fir, white cedar, and poplar, or alluvial flats clothed with cranberry and blueberry bushes; but the timber is of inferior quality, and the soil is poor. Bears, foxes, hares, martins, sea-otters, partridges, curlews, plovers and snipe are numerous. The geological formation is a shell limestone mixed with clay, and in some places wholly composed of encrinites. The northern coast is bold and high, presenting magnificent limestone cliffs, which sometimes rise to the height of 500 ft. The severity of the climate is so great that any grain is usually nipped in the bud. In the month of June ice of considerable thickness is frequently formed during the night; and summer is always far advanced before the snow disappears. Winter commences in the early part of November, and continues till the middle of March; the therm. ranging during this season from 20° above to 20° below zero. The shores are then surrounded with ice, and all communication with them is cut off. Its shores present a few small creeks, but throughout an extent of 300 m. there is neither bay nor harbour sufficient to protect ships; while the powerful stream setting constantly from the St. Lawrence, the shoals which surround this island, and the heavy snow-storms which here occur in the fall of the year, with its position across the mouth of the river, render it the frequent scene of shipwrecks, and the sailing past it "the worst part of the voyage to or from Canada." [Bonnycastle.] The bearings of its extreme points are as follows: west point, N lat. 49° 52′ 29", W long. 64° 36′ 54"; variation 22° 55' W.—East point, N lat. 49° 8' 30", W long. 61° 44′ 56"; variation 24° 38' W .- North point, N lat. 49° 57′ 38″, W long. 65° 14′ 1″.—South-west point, N lat. 49° 23', W long. 63° 43'. An elevated and magnificent lighthouse, with a revolving light, now occupies this point of the island; and another has been erected on the E coast.—Spring-tides rise 10 ft.; neaps, 4 ft.—This island formerly belonged to Labrador; but was annexed to Lower Canada in 1825, and now forms part of the co. of Saguenay. Its name is probably a corruption of the Indian name Naticostec. It is first noticed in 1535, by Cartier, who gave it the name of Assumption .- Bouchette .- Bonnycastle. — Macgregor. — United Service Journal.
ANTIFOFEN, a village of Switzerland, in the cant. of Lucerne, 5 m. N of Wellisau, on the l. bank of the Weggar, an affluent of the Aar.
ANTIGARCAH, an island in the Indian ocean, near the coast of Corcan, 30 m. N of Gheriah, in N lat. 17° 10′, E long. 72° 58′.
ANTIGL, a village of Bohemia, circle of Prachin, 10 m. S of Berg-Reichenstein, and 12 m. W of Winterberg; one of the places where the celebrated Bohemian plate-glass is manufactured.
ANTIGNAC, a commune of France, in the dep. of Cantal, cant. of Saigues. Pop. 1,828.
ANTIGNANA, a village of Illyria, gov. of Trieste, circle of Fiume, 36 m. WSW of Fiume. Pop. 1,199.
ANTIGNATE, a village of Lombardy, in the prov. of Bergamo, 17 m. SSE of Bergamo. Pop.

ANTIGNY, a town of Switzerland, cant. of Friburg, 8 m. SW of Friburg.-Also a village of France, in the Vendée, 10 m. NE of Fontenay-le-Comte. Pop. 1,300.—Also a commune in the dep. of Vienne, cant. of Saint-Savin. Pop. 1,120.

ANTIGUA, an island in the West Indies, belonging to Great Britain; called by the natives Xaymaca; by Columbus, Santa Maria de la Antigua. It is one of the Leeward islands; and is about 25 m. NE of Montserrat; 30 m. S of Barbuda; and 40 m. N of Guadaloupe. It is of an oval shape; its greatest length being about 20 m.; its circumference, about 54 m. The position of its cap., St. John, is N lat. 17° 10′, W long. 61° 57′. It contains an area of about 70,000 acres; of which about 60,000 are under cultivation. Of this 34,000 acres are appropriated to the growth of sugar; the other productions are cottonthe cultivation of which is now nearly abandoned— ginger, arrow-root, and tobacco. This island has neither fountain nor river, and is therefore exposed to frequent and very distressing droughts; at which times the only resource consists in a few trifling springs, and in the rain water-which has been preserved in tanks: the crops are on this account very uncertain. A. is considered not so healthy as some of the neighbouring islands, being very subject to hurricanes. The rainy season is generally from June to the end of the year. The temperature is tolerably uniform, resembling that of Barbadoes: the range of the thermometer being seldom more than 4° in 24 hours. In 1836 the mean temp. appears to have been 79.68; and the fall of rain 35.38. The average mortality of the regular troops is 40.5 per 1,000. Its geological constitution is of marl, conglomerate chert, and trap. A part of the island consists of a stratified green schist, which crosses it from N to S, in a zone of 3 or 4 m. width, and affords a useful building-stone [Maclure]. The soil is a reddish clay on a substratum of marl in the high lands; and a rich dark mould on a substratumof clay in the low lands. The surface is less mountainous than that of the other islands, the greatest elevation being only 1,210 feet. The rise of the surface is towards the S and SW: the highest land being in the vicinity of English harbour, and consisting of a succession of round-peaked hills, covered with small trees and brushwood, and sloping away in gentle undulations to the centre and windward parts. On the NE, a large portion of the island is low and swampy. From the sea, the coast presents a rugged and barren appearance, but a few miles inland the prospect is varied with the usual features of a tropical climate. "Antigua," says Bishop Coleridge, "on a larger scale is formed like Anguilla, that is, without any central eminences, but for the most part ramparted around by very magnificent cliffs which slope inwards in gradual declivities. From some of these rocks—especially near the parsonage of St. Philip's parish,—one of the finest panoramic views in the world may be obtained. The whole island, which is of a rough circular figure, lies in sight; the grand fortifications on the Ridge and Monk's hill silently menace the subject-fields; St. John's rises distinctly with its church on the north-western horizon; whilst the woods which cover the sides and crest the summit of Fig-tree hill just break the continuity of sea on the south-west. The heart of the island is verdant, with an abundant pasturage or grassy down; and the numerous houses of the planters, embosomed in trees, have more of the appearance of country mansions in England than almost any other in the West Indies. The shores are indented in every direction with creeks and bays and coves, -some of them running into the centre of Negroes. the plantations like canals, -some swelling into estuaries,-and others forming spacious harbours. Beyond these, an infinite variety of islands and islets stud the bosom of the blue sea, and stand out like so ANT waves. They are of all shapes and sizes, and are given up to the rearing of provisions and the maintenance of a great number of cattle. From the same hill, when the western sky is clear, Guadaloupe, Montserrat, Nevis, and St. Kitts may all be distinguished by the naked eye." A. contains the towns of St. John's, the capital, Parham, Falmouth, Willoughby bay, Old Road, and James Fort. St. John's, in the NW of the island, is regularly built, partly on a high rock connected with the mainland by a causeway which is submerged at high water. Its harbour affords sufficient depth for merchant-vessels, and perfect security from all winds; but English harbour, on the S side, is the best in A., and one of the best in all the West Indies, having water sufficient for the largest vessels, and being well-sheltered in all weathers: it has moreover a dock-yard, naval hospital, and every thing necessary for the refitting of vessels. Monk's hill, which rises from Falmouth harbour to an alt. of 625 ft., has on its summit an irregular fortress, covering 10 acres of ground, constructed to serve both as a citadel to the island, and a place of refuge in case of invasion. The pop. of the island, in 1774, amounted to 40,398, of whom 2,590 were whites, and 37,808 blacks. In 1824, it was returned at 36,860; in 1834, at 35,412; and in 1844, at 36,178: thus showing a tendency to decline, though the planters are said to have been remarkable for leniency towards their slaves. This island was the first which presented the example of an amelioration of the criminal law with regard to Negro slaves, by affording the accused party the benefit of trial by jury; and allowing, in case of capital convictions, four days to elapse between the time of sentence and the execution. The legislature of this island has likewise, in many other instances, displayed a singular measure of humanity and firmness. It totally abolished slavery in 1834, without requiring the stipulated apprenticeship; and in 1847, the governor reported that "here as in other islands, the material condition of the emancipated race is most satisfactory. They are abundantly supplied with all the necessaries, and many of the comforts of life. They are well-fed, well-housed, and well-clothed; the passion for fine dress in both sexes continues unabated. Through the aid of friendly societies, which are in active and beneficial operation, the poorest can command good medical attendance, and other privileges seldom enjoyed by persons in a similar rank of life in other countries. The number of labourers withdrawing from estates and settling in detached villages, where they purchase or rent small independent holdings, continues to increase. By returns made up to June of last year, there appears to be 70 settlements formed, containing about 3,300 houses, and a pop. of about 9,300. These village-communities are not peculiar to A., but owing to the transition which took place here direct from slavery to freedom, without the intervention of apprenticeship, they have made greater progress in this island, and from them may spring the germ of a middle class, which must exercise considerable influence over the future destinies of the colony." The governor adds that between 600 and 700 Portuguese emigrants, including men, women, and children, have been recently imported into the island from Madeira; and that they appear sober, industrious, and fully equal to the work of a sugar-plantation. Nor has any jealousy or ill-feeling been shown to them by the In 1812, the exports of A. were as follows: 13,625 hogsheads of sugar, 5,683 puncheons of rum, 74 casks of molasses, 19,480 lbs. of coffee, and 39,880 lbs. of cotton; valued at £389,740 sterling. The many advanced posts of defence against the invading | imports were estimated at £384,708. In 1832 there were exported 11,000 hogsheads of sugar, 7,342 puncheons of molasses, and 1,238 of rum. In 1836, the exports were 135,482 cwts. of sugar, 54,370 cwts. of molasses, 7,731 gallons of rum, 25,200 lbs. of arrow-root, and 1,010 lbs. of coffee. In 1846, they amounted to 102,644 cwts. of sugar, 46,205 cwts. of molasses, 41,110 gallons of rum, and 7,387 lbs. of coffee. The governor reported in 1847 that the returns exhibited a diminution both of imports and exports as compared with 1845. The estimated value of imports being £213,967 4s. 4d. in 1846, as against £267,237 in 1845; and of exports £177,520 10s. in 1846, against £295,492 10s. in 1845. "The falling-off in imports," he says, "appears to be chiefly attributable to a diminished quantity of supplies being introduced in the past year from the United States; arising partly, perhaps, from the more contracted demand for them than in previous years, when an unusual quantity of supplies of various kinds was required for the restoration of damages occasioned by the earthquake of 1843, and partly perhaps from the very short crop of 1846 causing money to be less freely circulated. While the great reduction in the exports has been occasioned by the very unfavourable weather experienced by the planters in taking off the last year's crop, calms prevailing in the early part of the season, and towards the end heavy and continuous rains; 7,171 hogsheads, 621 tierces, and 1,368 barrels of sugar only having been shipped in 1846, while in 1845 the export amounted to 11,805 hogsheads, 1,012 tierces, and 2,724 barrels. A considerable portion," he adds, "of last year's crop will consequently be computed in that of the present year, and it is expected that the average of the two years will be fully equal to the crop of 1845." The imports in 1835 amounted to £215,700; in 1841 to £215,529; in 1844 to £254,094. Of these the direct imports from Great Britain were in 1835, £124,405; in 1841, £112,496; in 1844, £144,223. The aggregate of exports in 1835 was £253,155, whereof £222,793 was to Great Britain; in 1841, £314,896, whereof £291,936 was to Great Britain; and in 1844 was £444,137, whereof £407,058 was to Great Britain. The number of vessels belonging to the island in 1844 was 55; total tonnage 833 tons. The legislature of A. is composed of a governor, a legislative council of 10 members who are nominated by the Crown, and a house-of-assembly of 25 members chosen by the freeholders. The governor—whose salary is £3,000, and whose general government extends to Dominica, Montserrat, Barbuda, Nevis, St. Kitt's, Anguilla, and the Virgin islands, but who is generally resident on this island—for the time being acts as chancellor of the court of equity. The suitors have a right of appeal from his decrees to the Privy council, on giving security for costs. There is a militia of about 1,000 men and officers; and a garrison of 200 Europeans and 100 Colonial regulars. The revenue in 1831 was £16,097; expenditure £15,708. In 1836 the revenue was £11,337; expenditure £16,249. The taxation averages 6s. 10d. per head. The property annually created is estimated at £1,000,000 per annum.—This island was discovered by Columbus in 1493. In 1632, a few English families took up their residence upon it, and began the cultivation of tobacco; but the number did not much increase for some years, until Lord Willoughby of Perham-to whom Charles II. in 1663 made a formal grant of A .- sent out a considerable number of inhabitants at his own expense. The settlement, however, was nearly strangled in its infancy; for, in 1666, a French armament from Martinico, with a body of Caribbs, invaded the island, and ravaged the country with fire and sword; but it was restored to the English in 1668, ANT

ANTIGUA, or OLD GUATEMALA, a town of Guatemala, about 9 leagues SW of the cap. It has been frequently desolated by earthquakes, and still presents "a splendid panorama of romantic dilapidation." Thomson, who visited it in 1825, is of opinion that it must at one time have covered an extent of ground as large as the present site of Mexico. Its pop. is about 12,000.—Also a town of the Philippine islands, on the W coast of Panay, in N lat. 11° 42', E long. 121° 0'. It has a safe roadstead.

ANTI-LIBANUS, a mountain-range of Asiatic Turkey, prov. of Syria, being an opposite and parallel chain to Libanus, running on the west of Damascus, and dividing the pashaliks of Damascus and Acre. The hollow country or long plain lying between these ranges is the ancient Calosyria. See article LIBANUS.

ANTILLES. A name variously applied by different geographers to portions of that extensive and important group of islands in the Atlantic, extending in a semicircular form between the two continents of America, and constituting a sort of barrier to the gulf of Mexico and the Caribbean sea. These islands describe nearly the diagonal of a parallelogram, of which the sides are the meridians of 59° and 86° W long., and the parallels of 10° and 20° N lat.; but this diagonal is of very variable breadth; and there are several small islands off the neighbouring coasts not included within the limits now traced. "These islands," says Malte Brun, "have been vaguely denominated the West Indies, from the term India originally given to America by Columbus. That illustrious navigator planned his voyage in the expectation of finding a western passage to India shorter and less tedious than that by the coast of Africa. This undertaking might have been accomplished had the geography of the ancients, on which it was founded, been correct; but although the discovery of the Pacific ocean detected the fallacy of Columbus, the islands still retained their ancient name." They are divided into the Greater and the Lesser Antilles; and have been nautically classified by the British under the general denominations of the WINDWARD and LEEWARD ISLES, the BAR-LO-VENTO and Soto- VENTO of the Spaniards. With reference to the trade-wind, however, the whole group are windward islands. The Greater Antilles, consisting of the four large islands of Cuba, Jamaica, St. Domingo or Hayti, the most southern of the windward group, and Porto Rico, the most northern of the Leeward, stretch from the coast of Florida on the W, towards the Lesser Antilles or Caribbee islands on the E, with which they are connected by the Virgin group. The Lesser Antilles, or Caribbean group—which have been subdivided likewise into Leeward and Windward, and to which Hoffman proposes to confine the term Antilles [quasi ante insulæ Americanæ] as lying in front of the larger islands-form a semicircular chain extending towards Cape Paria on the S, and then running in a westerly direction along the northern coast of South America to Cape Coquibacoa, on the W of the gulf of Maracaibo. The total number of islands and islets which compose these groups is upwards of eight hundred; but many of them are bare uninhabitable rocks. Divisions and Area.] With the exception of St. Domingo-which is now an independent republicthese islands belong to different European powers, as follow: Area in Pop. 2. Puerto Rico, with ditto, 3. Culebra and Bique, two of the Virgin islands, 1. Jamaica, . . . . 2. The Bahamas, . . . 12. Barbadoes, . . . *** ... IV. FRENCH ANTILLES. 1. Guadaloupe, with its depen- VI. DUTCH ANTILLES. 2,400 approx. 1. Saint Eustatia, . 3. St. Martin, ... 4. Curaçoa, . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . St. Bartholomew, ... . . Geological character. The Greater A. appear to be of primitive formation, and their highest summits are granite. Most of the Lesser A. have indications of volcanic origin; but in many of them the volcanic rocks are covered with calcareous formations of a thickness varying from 25 to 1,000 ft. Felspar, with a black substance resembling hornblende, constitutes a great proportion of the rocks of the volcanic islands. The volcanoes, to which these isles owe their origin, open their mouths chiefly towards the W, which side is usually rugged, and displays all the disorder incident to volcanic regions. Soufrieres, or spots which indicate the remains of a subsiding volcano, and whence hot sulphureous vapours are ejected, depositing sulphur as at Solfaterra near Naples, occur in many places. The great isolated rocks which shoot up in the sea at various distances around the A., with a bold and picturesque aspect, have been formed by submarine volcanoes. The most remarkable are at Saint Lucia, le Gros-Ilet; at Martinique, le Diamant, the isle of Ramiers, the Devil's table, the isle of Saint Aubin, the Caravelle, and the Perle; and at Guadaloupe, the isle of Goyave, the Caouenne, and the Grenada. The coasts of the A. are in general rugged and highly elevated; where low, they are bounded by thick swampy forests. In the former case, the adjacent sea is open and of a great depth, so that an anchorage is practicable only very close to the shore, above a bottom of black sand or rock; in the latter, the soundings show a muddy bottom, and coral-reefs compel ships to keep off the shore. This observation holds equally true of the whole archipelago of the A. Although the Caribbean islands have the appearance of a continuous chain, no bottom is found with a line of 100 or 150 fathoms, either between the larger islands or on their W or E sides. The tides are very irregular and uncertain: while at Jamaica the rise is scarcely perceptible; at Trinidad it amounts to 6 ft. Rivers and Lakes.] The rivers which flow from the cloud-capped summits of the mountains of the A. descend in a series of cascades to the sea. In the Lesser A., the course of a river is never below 2,550 yards, nor above six or seven leagues; but their depth is great, and their volume of water immense in winter. The proximity of the sun produces an immense evaporation from the Atlantic under the equator, and then the rains swell the streams to a prodigious and overwhelming size. The descent of these torrents may enable us to judge of their rapidity,-it is 6 or 9 inches in the toise, = 2.13 yards, in the least mountainous region of Guadaloupe. From barometrical observation, it appears that the river Cazenavrie in 120 toises. At all other times except when swollen, the rivers of the A. may be passed at the fords; and with which their bed is strewn. The only rivers of the A. which are navigable are those which mingle freely with the ocean; such are the Salée and Lamentine of Martinique; and the Caroni of Trinidad, isles of the archipelago are formed in the vestiges of ancient craters, or are contained between two projected basaltic currents. In the first case they occupy the conical or pyramidal summit of the mountains, presenting an elliptical basin; in the second, their themselves plentifully by subterranean as well as other fissures. In Guadaloupe we find the Grand- Roseau—there is an extensive lake, on the summit of a very high volcanic hill, which gives birth to several rivers. Winds, Climate, &c.] The N wind blows here from November to February, and sometimes lowers the mercury of the thermometer to 16° of Reaumur, or 69° of Fahrenheit, and the needle of the hygrometer to between 60° or 70°. Its prevalence is marked by epidemic rheumatism and catarrhic affections. The S wind is warm and humid; it blows from July to October, but with less force and continuity than that of the N and E. It raises the thermometer to 28° of Reaumur, and 95° of Fahrenheit. Its influence is dangerous and malignant. To it is attributable the exhalation from the marshes of Saint Lucia, and the elevation of the waters of the Orinoco, which rise to the height of 39 or 41 ft., and inundate the country 200 leagues E to W. The hurricane season is reckoned from the 15th of July to the 15th of October; and the hurricane region is included between the parallels of 10° and 28° N, and the meridians of 58° and 86° W. The east wind prevails in March, April, May, and June. It resembles the N, to which quarter it generally more or less inclines, but is not so dry or warm, for in traversing the Atlantic it loses, before reaching the A., a part of the heat it acquired in passing over the African deserts. During its continuance the climate is favourable to Creoles and Europeans. The W wind is the severest of all, and inclines more to the N than to the S. The seasons, however, alter with the winds, and are strictly confinable to two, - the wet season, from November to April, passing from S to E,—the dry from May to October, passing from E to S. Spring commences in April; and from May till October is the reign of summer, during which the medium height of the thermometer is about 80°. The autumnal rains commence in October, and continue till the middle of December: from 60 to 65 cubic inches are the medium fall. This humidity M. de Jonnes attributes, 1st, to the situation of the islands in the midst of a vast body of water, the daily evaporation from which amounts to more than 33,000,000 tons of water for a degree square; 2d, to the proximity of the different islands of the archipelago which form a chain of 200 leagues, disposed in the form of a right angle, the direction of which is towards the prevailing winds; 3d, to the mineralogical mass of these islands, which exercises a superior influence on the atmosphere to such insulated solitary islands as Saint Helena, Ascension, or the isle of Paquas; 4th, to the conflict between opposing currents of wind during the winter-season; 5th, to the elevation of the mountains, which rise 300 or 400 toises into the region of the clouds, which hang during the rainy season at less than 2,000 ft. above the level of the sea, beginning from the 14th parallel; and, 6th, to the conic or pyramidal form of the mountains, which sensibly augments their action on the electric clouds. -An official document on the health of the British troops stationed in the West Indies, exhibits, as regards British Guayana, Trinidad, Tobago, Grenada, St. Vincent's, Barbadoes, St. Lucia, Dominica, Antigna, and St. Kitt's, the following general results: Tobago is the most remarkable for fever; Dominica for diseases of the bowels and of the brain; Barbadoes for those of the lungs; Grenada for those of the liver; while Trinidad is most noted for its dropsies. It may be observed that the mortality of all the islands, except Antigua, Grenada, and Tobago, is higher among the troops than among the black population; and this is the more remarkable, as the mortality of the Negro slave population was calculated upon persons of all ages, including old men and infants, sickly and healthy; whereas that of the troops was calculated upon persons in the prime of life only. It appears that in these colonies, as well as in Jamaica, the most sickly as well as the most fatal period of the year extends from August to December, and that the only months comparatively healthy are March, April, and May. Vegetation.] The atmosphere of the A. resembles that of Africa more than that of Europe. Hence, while European productions degenerate here, those of Africa attain singular luxuriance. The sugar-cane—which now covers those islands—came originally from one of the African islands; the coffee, from Arabia; part of the alimentary plants, from the coast of Guinea; and the finest grapes of the savannahs, and flowers, from the same source. The dates are those of Atlas; and from Senegal were transplanted those tamarinds whose thick shade suffocates the American trees with which they are surrounded. The numerous race of Negroes too, originally brought from Africa, has here usurped the place of the aborigines. "Trees similar to those that we have admired in other tropical countries," says Malte Brun, "grow in equal luxuriance on these islands. A canoe made from a single trunk of the wild cotton-tree has been known to contain a hundred persons; and the leaf of a particular kind of palm-tree affords a shade to five or six men. The royal palmetto, or mountain-cabbage, grows to the extraordinary height of 200 ft.; and its verdant summit is shaken by the lightest breeze. Many of the plantations are enclosed by rows of Campeachy and Brazilian trees; the corab is alike prized for its thick shade and its excellent fruit; and the fibrous bark of the great cecropia is converted into strong cordage. The trees most valuable on account of their timber, are the tamarind, the cedar, the Spanish mountain-ash, the iron-tree, and the Laurus chloroxylon which is well-adapted for the construction of mills. The dwellings of the settlers are shaded by orange, lemon, and pomegranatetrees, which fill the air with the perfume of their flowers, while their branches are loaded with fruit. The apple, the peach, and the grape, ripen in the mountains. The date, the sapata, and sapotilla, the mammee, several Oriental fruits, the rose-apple, the guava, the munga, and different species of spondias and annonas grow on the sultry plains. Botanists have observed on the wide savannahs, the Serpidium Virginense, the Ocynium Americanum, the Cleomis pentaphyllon, and the Turnera pumicea. The coasts are shaded by phyleria, and every species of acacia, particularly the Farnese, remarkable for the beauty of its flowers. Opuntias and torch-thistles cover the sides of the mornes or precipices; and the vine-tree grows on the rocks in the neighbourhood of the shore. The woods abound in lianes, [Ficus Indica and radula,] whose branches, entwined around the trees, sometimes form verdant galleries or canopies of flowers. Silices arborescentes grow to a great height, and soon arrive at maturity. The Polypodium arboreum, which belongs to this class, may be mistaken at a distance for the palm-tree, on account of its lofty trunk and the broad leaves on its summit." The winter season is the great vegetating season in the West Indian islands. The sap then circulates with activity and energy,—the trees are almost at the same instant covered with flowers and fruit, mosses and lichens cover the walls,-and stramoniums of gigantic size and purple euphorbiums spring up in the unfrequented paths. ANT The Sugar-cane.] Sugar is the great staple commodity of the West Indies. To this day, it is not exactly known what country the sugar-cane was originally imported from; but it is generally believed that it came from the East Indies. In the 12th or 13th cent. it was transplanted into Sicily; whence it was taken to Madeira, then recently discovered by the Portuguese. About the same time the Spaniards introduced it into the Canary islands. Attempts were made to plant it in Provence; but they did not succeed. In the beginning of the 17th cent., France had no sugar but what came from Madeira and the Canaries; but towards the end of the cent., the English had monopolized this article of trade, and all the north of France was in general supplied with sugar from England. From the Canary islands, the cane was conveyed to the American continent and islands, and afterwards to Madagascar, the coasts of Coromandel and Malabar, Ceylon and Manilla, and, at length, even to Otaheite. The sugar-cane is propagated by grains or seed. There are several varieties of this plant. One of these, which is white, with a thin bark, and knots at spaces five fingers in length, is very productive both of juice and sugar. A second species is of a reddish colour; its knots lie nearer together; its bark is hard; and its produce of sugar less considerable but sweeter. In a third species, the stalk is not above an inch thick; the bark is thin, the flutings are green, the knots very distant: this last has a very sweet taste, and yields a great quantity of sugar. All the three species ripen in nine or ten months. Animal kingdom.] This archipelago, forming an arc between North and South America, is connected with both in its animal as well as vegetable kingdoms. "It has been observed by travellers," says Malte Brun, "that most of the wild animals indigenous to the West Indies are of a small size, as the Vespertilio molussus, the Viverra caudivolvula, and the Mus pilorides. Lizards, and different kinds of serpents, are not uncommon, but the greater number of them are harmless; and, with the exception of Martinique and St. Lucia, no scorpions are to be found in the Lesser A. This noxious reptile, however, is frequently observed in Porto Rico, and it exists probably in all the larger islands." Scarabæus Hercules exists in the greater part of the archipelago. The most destructive animal which infests the West Indies is the rat. It was introduced into several of the West India islands about sixty years ago, by Sir Charles Price, for the purpose of extirpating the native rat. This it soon did most effectually, but at the same time overran the island itself, proving by a thousand degrees a greater pest than its predecessors. It annually does great damage to the cane-grounds. One of the first animals which attracts the attention of a stranger in the West Indies, is the large carrion-crow, called by the Negroes 'the John-crow.' It is a large, heavy, sluggish bird, about the size of a British turkey, the head exactly resembling that of the latter. The parrot and its various congeners, from the macaw to the parroquet, frequent the forests; and aquatic birds in unnumbered flocks enliven the shores. The colibry or humming-bird is the sportive inhabitant of these warm climes; it seldom remains long in the same place, but is seen for a moment on the blossoms of the orange or lime-tree, and displays in its golden plumage the brightest tints of the emerald and the ruby. Authorities.] Edwards' History of the West Indies. -Humboldt, Voyage aux Regiones equinoctiales.-Histoire physique des Antilles par M. Moreau de Jonnès.— Maclure's Observations on the Geology of the West India Islands.—R. G. S. Journal.—Parliamentary papers.—Malte Brun.

ANTI-MI'LO, an uninhabited island in the Greek archipelago, one of the Cyclades. The summit of its most elevated peak is in N lat. 36° 48', E long. 24° 13'. It is 5 m. NW of Cape Vani, the western extremity of Port Milo, in the island of Milo, and a sailing-mark in approaching that harbour.
