ANTIMO, a town of Naples, in the district of Casoria, 7 m. N of Naples. Pop. 7,000.
ANTIN, a commune and town of France, dep. of the Upper Pyrenees, arrond. of Tarbes, 60 m. WSW of Toulouse, and 17 m. ENE of Tarbes. Pop. 479.
ANTINOE. See ABABDEH.

ANTIOCH, called ANTAKI, or ANTAKIEH, by the Turks, a city of Syria, in the pashalik of Haleb, on the l. or S bank of the Asy or Orontes, which is here from 100 to 150 ft. wide, 46 m. W of Aleppo, and 22 m. S of Scanderun on the Mediterranean; in N lata 36° 6', E long. 36° 18'. It lies in a direction from ENE to WSW, on a long narrow slip rising in a gentle slope, between the Orontes and the base of a mountain, with the great plain of A. immediately in front, and the lake of Antioch or Ufrenus towards the NE. It is surrounded by walls enclosing a space above a mile in diameter. The walls-which are from 30 to 50 ft. high-are flanked by strong square towers at unequal distances. Only a small portion of the original city, of which the extent is ascertained by the line of wall, is contained within their present enclosure; and A. in its modern state does not occupy one-eighth part of the space included by the old walls. [Mangles.] The remainder of the space within the ancient circumvallation is covered with plantations of mulberry, apricot, olive, and pomegranate trees. The streets are narrow, with elevated footpaths on each side; the houses are built of stone, pent-roofed, and covered with red tiles, which is unusual in Syria; and generally consist of two stories with a square in the centre, but without terraces. The bazaars are numerous. This city is governed by a mutsellim, dependent on the pasha of Aleppo, but appointed by the Porte. It is also the residence of a patriarch of the Greek church. The pop. is supposed to consist of about 10,000 Mahommedans, 3,000 Christians, and 150 Jews. Kinneir estimated it in 1813 at 10,000. In the reign of Theodosius, it contained 100,000 Christians alone. The language chiefly spoken is the Turkish. From the highest part of the rocks within the fortifications, and rather nearer the W than the E side, a most magnificent and extensive view may be obtained. "To the E is the great plain of Antioch, with its lake, bounded by distant mountains; the Orontes, winding through the plain in front of the city; the high mountains of Beilan; the sea on the SW; Mount Casius; the irregular valley covered with vineyards, behind the heights of the city: these are the chief objects which strike the beholder from the highest point of the ancient capital of Syria. Mount Casius is of a conical form. The Orontes, after passing A., takes its course between some low mountains N of Mount Casius, and discharges itself into the sea, about 18 m. from A." [Squire.] The Orontes was at one time navigable up to the city, but is now so full of rapids, weeds, islands, and shoals, as to be impracticable even for a boat. The remains of the port now form a marsh of about 5 acres, with a massive wall encircling it. The manufactures of A. are silks, coarse cotton cloth, pottery, goats' wool, leather, and saddlery. A large species of eel which is caught in great numbers in the Orontes, and in the lake of A., supplies an important article of commerce, being salted and exported in every direction for the use of the Greeks during their fasts before Easter to the value, it is said, of 60,000 piastres yearly. The appearance of the country around A. is picturesque, verdant, and delightful,-"a territory unrivalled for richness, beauty, and variety of feature." [Kinneir.] The trees in the vicinity of A. chiefly consist of the myrtle, the oak, the sumach, the mulberry, and the fig. A great quantity of grapes is produced in the neighbourhood; but the staple production is silk, of which A. and the environs produce annually about 500 cantars, or nearly one-third of the total produce of Syria. The growth of cotton amounts to from 100 to 150 cantars. The city of A. was originally founded by Antigonus, who succeeded Alexander in Syria; and was called Antigonia by him. Seleucus, after vanquishing Antigonus, with the materials of the town built another city, which he named Antiochia after his father. A. was long celebrated as one of the first cities of the East. "A. and Alexandria," says Gibbon, "looked down with disdain on a crowd of dependent cities, and yielded with reluctance to the majesty of Rome itself." It was the residence of the Macedonian kings of Syria, and subsequently of the Roman governors of Syria. In the contests which took place between the rival empires of Rome and Persia, A. was, by its situation, exposed to the invad- ANT 242, by Sapor king of Persia: and on two subsequent occasions was attacked and taken by the same monarch, who levelled all its public edifices to the ground. In the 6th cent. it again fell into the hands of the Persians, by whom it was sacked and plundered; in 588 it was devastated by an earthquake in which 60,000 souls perished; and in 634 it was taken by the Saracens, from whom it was recovered by the Romans, in whose possession it remained until conquered by the Turks. The Crusaders having rendered themselves masters of this part of Syria towards the close of the 11th cent., this city and the surrounding district constituted a Christian principality under the name of Antioch. In 1269, the city was captured by Bendocdar, sultan of Babylon: on which occasion 15,000 citizens were killed, and 100,000 carried into captivity. A. was afterwards incorporated with the Turkish empire, but it never regained its pristine celebrity. In 1822 it was desolated by an earthquake. Ibrahim Pasha took this city in his campaign of 1832. -Parsons. Volney. Kinneir. Bowring's Commercial Statistics.
ANTIOCHETTA, the ancient Antiocheia ad Cra gum, a town of Asiatic Turkey, on the SW coast of the pashalik of Itchil, and sanjak of Selevke, 26 m. WNW of Cape Anamur, and 10 m. SSE of Selinty.

ANTIOCO (ISOLA SANT'), an island near the SW extremity of Sardinia, the ancient Enosis and Plumbaria, belonging to the prov. of Cagliarli. It is about 11 m. in length; from 3 to 6 in breadth; and 26 m. in circuit. Its general geological character is volcanic. Extensive salt marshes occur in the vicinity of Calasetta. The chief town-also called Sant' Antioco-is situated on the E coast of the island, on the gulf of Palmas. It is of considerable strength, and possesses a small naval port. Its pop. is estimated at 1,800. The E coast of Sant' A. forms the W side of the gulf of Palmas. This island at the period of the Roman supremacy was extremely populous; and ample evidence of its early importance exists in the valuable and numerous relics of antiquity which it contains. After the wars of the Pisans and Genoese it sunk into comparative insignificance.

ANTIOQUIA, a prov. of New Granada, stretching along the basin of the Cauca, between the provinces of Popayan on the S and Carthagena on the N, extending on the NW to the Caribbean sea and separated from the Pacific on the W by the prov. of Choco. It is generally mountainous, well watered, and fertile, and contains some valuable gold mines. The cap., of the same name, sometimes called Santa

FE DE ANTIQUIA, is situated on the l. bank of the Cauca about 35 m. NW of Medellin, and 183 NW of Santa Fe de Bogota. It is the see of a bishop. In its environs are considerable gold-mines, and the sugar-cane is extensively cultivated. Pop. 19,000.

ANTIPARO, or ANTIPAROS, an island of the Grecian archipelago, in the Cyclades group, between Paros and Siphanto, at the distance of about 2 m. from the former; in N lat. 37° and E long. 25° 3'. It is a mass of white marble, about 7 m. in length; and varies from 2 to 3 m. in breadth. Its principal productions are cotton, wine, and barley. This island, the Oliaros of antiquity, is chiefly remarkable for a grotto whose entrance is on a hill about half-a-mile from the beach, presenting at its entrance a rocky cavity opening by a passage, long, narrow, and of rapid descent, into an expanse 180 ft. in height and 360 in breadth, hung on all sides with gigantic stalactites pendant from the roof, or formed upon the floor, in diversified forms of columns, cones, and curtains. Tournefort calls it a "vegetation of stones;" and describes it in somewhat exaggerated ing armies. It was taken and plundered, in the year | language. It seems to have been unknown to the ancients. The greatest depth of this natural excavation is 1,400 ft. A. was the birth-place of Phidias and Praxiteles. There is a small village of about 250 inhabitants on the island. To the SW of it are the barren islets of Strongylo and Spotico. ANTI'-PAXO, ANTI-PAXOS, or ANTI-PASSO, one of the Ionian isles, about 1 m. SE of the island of Paxo, from which it is separated by a deep though narrow channel; in N lat. 39° 8' 20" and E long. 20° 15'. It is about 2 m. in length from NW to SE, and uninhabited; but the olive, vine, and various other species of fruit are successfully cultivated on it by the inhabitants on the adjacent islands. A. belongs to the Ionian republic.
ANTIPINSK, or Antininsk, a town of Russia in the gov. of Vologda, on the l. bank of the Luza about 35 m. SE of Lalsk.

ANTIPODES, the name given by geographers and astronomers to those inhabitants of the earth who occupy antipodal points of the earth's surface, that is, points diametrically opposite to each other, and who of course turn their feet towards each other. The name is from the Greek avti, against, and Tous, a foot. The antipodes live in similar but opposite latitudes; and their longitudes differ 180° if reckoned round the globe; but if we distinguish E and Wlong. must together make up 180°. Hence, while they enjoy a like climate so far as that is dependent on latitude, the difference in their days is about 12 hours, that is, when it is noon at one place, it is midnight at its antipodal point; and their seasons too are reversed. The spherical form of the earth naturally leads us to the idea of the antipodes, of whose existence some idea was entertained even before the age of Cicero. The fathers of the church, however, found in this theory a contradiction of the Bible; and, in the 8th cent., Virgilius, archbishop of Saltzburg, was excommunicated for maintaining it! Circumnavigators of the globe first put the question beyond all doubt; and the opposition to the doctrine of the sphericity of the earth, and the existence of the antipodes, has long since died away.

ANTIPODES ISLAND, a small island in the S Pacific ocean, SSE of New Zealand, in S lat. 49°. It is the nearest land to the antipodes of Greenwich, which is in the open sea, in S lat. 51° 28' and long. 180°.

ANTIPOVKA, a village of Russia in the gov. of Saratov, on the Volga, in the district and 21 m. SSW of Kamishin. It is inhabited by a colony of Don Cossacks. In the vicinity are extensive limestone quarries.
ANTI-PSARA. See IPSARA.

ANTISANA, a volcano in the Andes, in the republic of Ecuador, 39 m. SE of Quito; in S lat. 0° 29' 45", W long. 78° 241'. In 1802, the crater of this mountain was ascertained by Humboldt to be elevated 19,137 ft. above the level of the sea. At that time smoke was issuing from several openings upon it; but it has made no considerable eruptions since 1590. -There is a village of the same name, elevated, according to Humboldt, 3,800 ft. above the plain of Quito, and 13,500 ft. above the level of the sea. It is the highest inhabited spot in the world. In the subjoined sketch from Humboldt, A represents the mountain; B the ridge of Chussolongo; c Lake Chussolongo; d the site of the village of A.; C Prussia, prov. of Cleves and Berg, circle of what appears to have been the basin of a former lake. 17 m. WNW of Dusseldorf. Pop. 1,985. ANT

ANTI-TAURUS, an extensive chain of mountains in Asiatic Turkey, one of the most considerable ramifications of Mount Taurus, which it joins in Caramania, near the sources of the Kizil-Irmak. It commences to the NE of the Cilician pass; and runs E and NE, separating the plain of El-Bostan from that of Cæsarea-Mazaca, and passing NE to the source of the Northern Halys, E of Sivas, whence it runs E to the Euphrates. The upper valley of the Northern Halys is formed by the two parallel ranges of the Anti-Taurus and the Paryadres,—the former on the south, and the latter on the north, -whilst the apex of the valley is at the junction of the two ranges. The Anti-Taurus may very properly be denominated the Northern Taurus, as it crosses the Euphrates, and runs eastward through Armenia, separating the valley of the Morad, or southern branch of the Euphrates, from that of the Karasu, or northern branch.

ANTIVARI, a kadilik and town of Albania, in the pashalik of Scutari. The kadilik comprises the greater part of the isthmus between the Adriatic and the lake of Scutari. The town is situated on a small river of the same name, 3 m. from the Adriatic, 12 m. N of Dulcigno, and 19 m. WNW of Scutari. Pop. 3,000. It is defended by a strong castle, which stands on the summit of a precipitous rock, and is the entrepot of the commerce of the valley of the Drino. It has a small harbour at the mouth of the river, in N lat. 42° 3', with good anchorage in the bay, which is 8 m. N of Point Gisana.

ANTOGAST, or Maisach, a village of Baden, in the circle of Kinzig, at the base of the Knubis, 11 m. E of Offenburg. Its mineral waters are celebrated, and form a considerable article of export. Pop. 414.
ANTOGIES, a village of Belgium, prov. of East Flanders, on a stream which joins the Scheldt 9 m- SW of Oudenarde.
ANTOIGNE', a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Maine-et-Loire, cant. of Montreuil-Bellay. Pop. 542.
ANTOINE (SAINT), a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Isère, cant. of Saint-Marcellin. Pop. 3,000.—Also a com. in Dordogne, cant. of Vélines. Pop. 1,520.
ANTOING, a town of Belgium, prov. of Hainault, 4 m SE of Tournay, on the r. bank of the Scheldt. It is built on a rising ground overlooking the famous plain of Fontenoy. Pop. in 1846, 2,395.
ANTOLIN, a village of Prussian Poland, in the gov. of Masovia, circle of Rawa. Pop. 400.

ANTON, or Test, a river which rises 10 m. N of Andover in Hampshire, and after receiving several streams at Redbridge, and flowing 35 m., joins the Itchin and takes the name of Southampton-water, which it retains until it joins the Solent, or channel which separates the isle of Wight from the mainland. —Also a town of Brazil, between the Chiru and Colorado rivers.—Also a town of Brazil, in the prov. of Paraiba, on the Camurataba.
ANTON (SANCT), a town of Hungary, in the circle of Honth, 4 m. SE of Schemnitz. Pop. 1,500.

ANTONGIL, a bay on the E coast of Madagascar, between lat. 15° 22' and 16° 10' S. At the bottom of the bay are several small islands, the principal of which, Marotto, is about a mile in length, and the same space from the shore. The French have attempted a settlement in this bay, called Port Choiseul, with an anchorage in a river the mouth of which bears NNW from Marotto.
ANTONI, a town of the Morea, on the W coast of the gulf of Kolokythia, 14 m. NNE of Maina, in N lat. 36° 43'.
ANTONI IN DER HEYDE (St.), a town of Prussia, prov. of Cleves and Berg, circle of Kempen,
ANTONIE (Sr.), a town of Belgium, prov. of Gueldres, district of Gomert, 17 m. S of Nimeguen. Pop. 1,700.—Also a town in North Brabant, 25 m. E of Bois-le-duc.
ANTONIEN (SANCT), a village of Switzerland, in the Grisons, 20 m. NE of Coire, on the Dalvatza, an affluent of the Landquart, which flows through a picturesque valley 9 m. in length.

ANTONIN (SAINT), a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Tarn-et-Garonne, arrond. of Montauban. Pop. of cant., comprising 8 com., in 1831, 13,995.—The com. and town are at the confluence of the Aveyron and the Bonnette, in an extensive valley surrounded by lofty mountains. Pop. 5,500. Manufactories of serges, baizes, and other woollen goods exist here; also paper-mills; but the chief trade is in leather of all kinds. Plums and saffron are grown in great quantities in the vicinity.
ANTONINA, a village of Brazil, prov. of San Paolo, 9 m. W of Paranagua, at the estuary of the united streams of the Cachoeira and Nundiaguara. It has an export-trade in cassada, cordage, and timber.

ANTONINOV, a village of Russian Poland, gov. of Sandomir, district of Opoczno. Iron-foundries and forges are established here, which produce annually 5,000 quintals of pig-iron, and 4,000 of bar-iron.

ANTONINUS (WALL OF), a military work erected by Lollius Urbicus, about A. D. 140, across the isthmus between the estuaries of the Forth and Clyde, to protect the districts to the south from the incursions of the Caledonians. It was afterwards called Grime's dyke, or Græme's dyke. The wall was of earth, 36 m. in length, with a foss 15 ft. wide, connecting the chain of stations established by Agricola, A. D. 80. The plough has obliterated it in most places, but a considerable portion of it may still be traced, especially in the neighbourhood of Falkirk in Stirlingshire.
ANTONIO ALVES, a river of Brazil, prov. of Rio Grande do Sul. It receives the Doudarilho river, about 10 m. above its junction with the Jacuhy, which it falls into near Portalegre.
ANTONIO (SAN), the name of above 100 villages in South America, most of which are too inconsiderable to require special notice.

ANTONIO (SAN), the northernmost of the Cape Verd islands. Its NE point is in N lat. 17° 15', W long. 24° 51'. Pop. 1,000, nearly all Negroes and Mulattoes. It produces corn, bananas, figs, guavas, grapes, oranges, sugar-cane, and cocoa-nuts. Poverson is the cap. The principal port is Point-de-Sol. Ships, not wishing to touch at the Cape de Verd islands, should keep at least 30 m. to the W of San A., so as to avoid the frequent calms which take place within 10 or 15 m. of this lofty island, of which some of the highest summits—which are constantly enveloped in dense clouds-attain an elevation of 7,400 ft., and which is in fact an immense mass of piles of lava,—here rising in sugar-loaf summits, there jagged and rent, in some places forming regular ridges, in others broken into deep ravines and vallies. Towards the S coast, the mountains gradually shelve down to the sea, presenting a fertile unbroken declivity studded with sugar and coffee plantations.

ANTONIO DE BEXAR (SAN), originally the cap. of Texas, on the San A. river, in N lat. 29° 50', W long. 101°. It probably contained at one time a pop. of 10,000 or 12,000; but the many fierce battles which have been fought within its walls during the revolutionary struggle, have reduced it to one-half that number. It is laid out and built with considerable regularity. The houses are all of one story, with few windows, and thick walls.
ANTONIO DE LOS JUES (SAN), a populous town of Mexico, on the road from Orizaba to Oaxaca, 45 m. N of the latter town, celebrated for its remains of ancient fortifications.
ANTONIO DELLA FLORIDA (SAN), a town of Chili, in the prov. of Maule, in S lat. 30° 20'.

ANTONIO (RIO DE SAN), the name of several ANT rivers in Brazil, one of which joins the Paracuto in S lat. 17°; another flows into the Atlantic in S lat. 9° 30'; another joins the Rio Grande de Curituba in S lat. 26°; another falls into the Ignassu after a course of 150 m. from SE to NW; another joins the Rio Dolce after a course of about 200 m.—Also a small river of Buenos Ayres, which enters the Parana.- Also a river of Costa Rica, in Guatemala.—Also a river in Texas, which takes its rise about 3 m. to the NE of San Antonio de Bexar, and is navigable by canoes to its source. At the town of San A. it is about 60 ft. wide, and in some places 12 ft. deep. It discharges itself into the Guadaloupe.

ANTONIO (CAPE SAN), a high and steep headland on the coast of Valencia, in Spain; in N lat. 38° 49' 50", E long. 0° 8'. It shelters the bay of Xaira from S and SW winds.—Also the extreme W point of the island of Cuba, in N lat. 21° 51′ 40", W long. 84° 55'. The entrance into the gulf of Mexico between this cape and Cape Catoche, the most salient extremity of Yucatan, is 38 leagues wide.
ANTONIO (PORT), a harbour 6 m. W of the NE end of Jamaica, in N lat. 18° 14' 40", W long. 76° 31'. The adjacent country is rich and beautiful.
ANTONIWALD, a village of Bohemia, in the circle of Bunzlau, 4 m. NNE of Morchenstern. Pop. 370. It is celebrated for its glass manufactures.
ANTONNE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Dordogne, cant. of Savignac. Pop. 900.

ANTONY, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of the Seine, 6 m. SSW of Paris. Pop. 1,300. Wax-bleacheries and manufactories of wax candles are established here; and in the vicinity are quarries of building-stone.

ANTONY WEST, or St. Jacob's, a parish of Cornwall, 13 m. SW of Devonport. Area 2,860 acres. Pop. 2,894. The proximity of Plymouth and Devonport, the dock-yards, public offices, &c., adds much to the population and prosperity of this place.
ANTRAIGUES, a picturesque village, commune, and canton of France, in the Vivarais, dep. of Ardèche, 13 m. W of Privas. Pop. of the com. 1,908; of the cant., comprising 8 com., 9,702 in 1831.

ANTRAIN, a canton, commune, and village of France, in the dep. of Ille-et-Vilaine, arrond. of Fougères. Pop. of cant., which comprises 10 com., in 1831, 15,980; of the com. 1,742. Leather-making and shoe-making employ a considerable proportion of the inhabitants of the village.

ANTRIM, a maritime co. of Ireland, forming the NE extremity of the prov. of Ulster. It is bounded on the N by the Atlantic; on the E by the North channel; on the SE and S by Down; and on the W by Tyrone and Londonderry. Its boundary over all the SE and S, excepting 5 m. adjacent to Lough- Neagh, is formed by Belfast-lough and the river Lagan; and, over all the W, excepting 7 m. adjacent to the ocean, by Loughs Neagh and Beg, and the river Bann. The co. is thus nearly insulated between a sweep of the sea and an alternate chain and line of fresh water. It lies between lat. 54° 26' and 55° 12′ 16" N, and long. 5° 40′ and 6° 37' W. Its greatest length, from Bengore-head on the N, to Spener's-bridge on the S, is 413 m.; its greatest breadth, from the Gobbins on the E, to Island Reagh Troone on the W, is about 24 m.; and its area, exclusive of the co. of the town of Carrickfergus, is 745,177 acres. The area is physically distributed into 53,288 ac. of water, 1,908 ac. of towns, 10,358 ac. of plantations, 176,335 ac. of waste mountain and bog, and 503,288 ac. of arable land. The whole land-surface may be regarded as proximately onethird upland, and two-thirds dale and valley and undulating expanse. The mountains—or rather hills, for they are moderate in alt.—commence 5 m. SW by S of Belfast, and | about the same distance NNE of the most southerly point in the county; and they extend in an almost unbroken chain round the coast to the boundary with Londonderry. The principal summits are Slamish, about the middle, and Knocklead, in the north; both of which have an altitude of less than 1,600 ft. above sea-level. Among the heights which run sheer out to the sea, Fair-head has an elevation of 636 ft., Garronpoint 764, Ballygally - head 797, Carrowmurphy 819, and Lurtgethon 1,154.—The principal rivers are the Six-mile-water, the Glenwherry, the Braid, the Ravel, the Bush, the Bann, the Lagan, the Carey, the Glenshesh, and some other minor streams. Loughs Neagh and Beg are the two principal lakes. Geology, Soil, and Agriculture.] The primary rock district of the county is confined to a small mountaingroup in the extreme NE. The coal-district is situated on the N coast, both E and W of Fair-head. The collieries on both sides of Fair-head, though now neglected, were wrought at a very remote period, and possibly may be among the most ancient in the British empire. Very nearly the whole of the remaining part of the county is occupied by upper members of the secondary series, capped by a thick mass of tabular trap. The lias limestone is 50 feet thick at Larne, and about 30 in Colin-glen, 3 m. SW of Belfast; it varies in solidity as in thickness, and contains a profusion of ammonites, pecten, mya, dentalia, plagiostoma, and other orders of organic remains. Magnesian limestone, averaging, perhaps, 60 feet in thickness, varies in colour from yellow to dull grey, and is interstratified with red and yellow sandstone. Traces of the oolitic formation have been observed nowhere except on the coast near Larne. The trap of A. has attracted much attention. The great range of columnar basalt, 45 ft. thick, which appears 100 ft. above high-water at Bengore-head, dips towards the W, and forms the basaltic pavement of the Giant's Causeway by its intersection with the plane of the sea.—A strong loam, interspersed in many places with the debris and small boulders of trap, and generally superincumbent on clay, is the prevailing soil in the plains and valleys. As the ground rises, the vegetable mould becomes shallower in depth, looser in texture, and lighter in colour, and the subsoil loses much of its tenacity, and degenerates into brown or yellow till. Peat or turf, too, soon succeeds to all vestige of the loam; stretches away, over the summits or platforms of the mountains, in extensive tracts of moss; and forms bogs, of various extent and density, which, while they offer a coarse summer herbage to sheep or cattle, seem to bid defiance to the inroads of the georgical improver.-In 1841, the total number of farms, exclusive of those in the co. of the town of Carrickfergus, was 23,526; and of these 6,855 measured from 1 acre to 5 acres each, -10,563, from 5 to 15 acres each, -4,220, from 15 to 30 acres each,—and 1,888, upwards of 30 acres. Wheat is very generally cultivated; but oats are the predominant cereal produce; their grain, jointly with potatoes, forms the chief food of the people, and their straw the chief support of the cattle. In 1847, the quantity of land under wheat amounted to 10,336 acres; oats, to 107,615; barley, 5,280; potatoes, 14,395; turnips, 14,414; flax, 2,516. The total produce of grain amounted to 95,902 tons. Flax is a prominent crop, in very fluctuating demand; so that the quantity of seed annually imported for it into Belfast was at one time 35,000 bushels, at another comparatively trifling; in 1835 upwards of 108,000 bushels, and in 1843, 93,429. Potatoes are of course a large object of attention.-The total number, and the estimated value of live stock, in 1841, was 22,651 horses and mules, £181,208; 69,638 cattle, £452,647; I. 18,150 sheep, £19,965; 32,882 pigs, £41,102; 153,672 poultry, £3,842; and 145 asses, £145. Total value of the whole live stock, £698,909. Manufactures, &c. ] The linen manufacture, long so prominent in A., belongs in a loose sense to about one-half of Ireland, and emphatically to the prov. of Ulster. The ordinary linens of A. rarely or never exceed 36 inches. Fine yard-wide cambrics, lawns, and diapers, are made in the district along Belfastlough and the Lagan. The cotton manufacture was projected at Belfast in 1777; it has been doubled in extent since 1800; it now employs from 25,000 to 27,000 persons in the district along Belfast-lough and the Lagan. Stockings and coarse woollen cloth are very generally woven throughout the county; soap and candles have been bulkily produced for exportation; leather was, at one time, manufactured in almost every town; the casting of iron makes some figure in Belfast. Ship-building has been considerably carried on at Belfast both in wood and iron; and saddlery, scythe-stones, and various articles for home-use, compose a large and diffusive miscellaneous manufacture. The interior traffic of the county, while large, animated, and very various, does not easily admit of being estimated. The overland traffic with other counties is chiefly concentrated in an exterior direction at Dublin, Armagh, and Coleraine, and, in an interior direction, at Belfast. The Ulster railway will afford communication from the commercial metropolis of A. to Armagh; and a projected line will continue the communication southward to a junction at Navan with the line from Enniskillen to Dublin. The roads of the county are in general excellent. The only interior navigation is that of the Lagan. Divisions.] By far the greater part of Antrim, from the north coast southward, formed for a series of centuries the ancient kingdom of Dalriada. At a subsequent period, the territory from Belfast-lough and the Lagan, westward to Lough-Neagh, was called North Claneboy, in contradistinction to South Claneboy in Down. The division according to which all taxes upon the county are now apportioned, consists of the county of the town of Carrickfergus, and the 14 baronies of Lower Antrim, Upper Antrim, Lower Belfast, Upper Belfast, Carey, Lower Dunluce, Upper Dunluce, Lower Glenarm, Upper Glenarm, Kilconway, Lower Massarene, Upper Massarene, Lower Toome, and Upper Toome.—In ecclesiastical position and arrangement, the county nearly coincides with the diocese of Connor. Of 76 parishes into which the co. is divided, only that of Aghalee is not in Connor; and of all the parishes within the dio., only those of the Liberties of Coleraine are not in Antrim. - Carrickfergus, Belfast, Antrim, and Lisburn are corporate towns. The annual amount of the valuation of the county, under 6 and 7 Will. IV., but exclusive of the co. of the town of Carrickfergus, is £474,524 17s. 2d. The amount of county-cess levied for local purposes, in 1824, was £32,094; in 1834, £45,612; in 1838, £53,256.—The county itself sends two members to parliament; and, including Belfast, Lisburn, and Carrickfergus, six members for a total pop. of 360,875. Constituency, in 1835, 3,933; in 1841, 2,157; of whom 268 were £50 freeholders, 213 were £20 freeholders, 1,469 were £10 freeholders, 33 were £20 leaseholders, 165 were £10 leaseholders, and 9 were £20 rent-chargers. The population of the county in 1821, was 262,860; in 1831, 372,938; in 1841, 360,875. Families, in 1831, 59,959; in 1841, 50,910. In 1841, the total of inhabited houses was 47,880, of uninhabited houses 2,674. The total of families chiefly employed in agriculture was 27,174, in manufactures and trades 20,239, and in other pursuits 3,497; the total of families chiefly dependent on property and professions was 1,089, on the directing of labour 20,233, on their own manual labour 28,910, and on means not specified 678.

ANTRIM, a parish, partly in the SE corner of the barony of Upper Toome, but chiefly on the southern border of the barony of Upper Antrim, co. Antrim. Length, <math>4\frac{1}{2}</math> miles; breadth, <math>4\frac{1}{4}</math>; area, 8,361 acres, exclusive of 523 acres in Lough-Neagh. Pop. in 1831, 5,415; in 1841, 5,182. Houses 955.

ANTRIM, a post and market town, the capital of two baronies, the nominal but unreal capital of its county, and formerly a parliamentary borough, stands on the r. bank of the Six-mile-water, 3 of a mile above the river's influx to Lough-Neagh, 134 m. NW of Belfast, and 105 m. N of Dublin. Pop. in 1831, 2,655; in 1841, 2,393; and, with a suburb called Massarene, 2,645. Though well-situated for commanding the trade on Lough-Neagh, and serving as a depot and central market to an extensive agricultural district, it enjoys little consideration, and has lost nearly all of the ancient consequence which rendered it a successful competitor with Carrickfergus for the metropolitan honours of the county.

ANTRIM, a township in Hillsborough co., New Hampshire, U. S., 28 m. W by S of Concord. It is *atered by the Contocook and North Branch rivers. Pop. 1,225.—Also a township in Guernsey co., Ohio; and another in Franklin co., Pennsylvania. Pop. 4,061.
ANTROBUS, a township in the parish of Great Budworth, Cheshire, 5 m. NNW of Northwich. Area 2,560 acres. Pop. 489.
ANTROBUS (CAPE), a headland on the SW coast of Baffin's bay, in N lat. 71° 35′, W long. 73°.
ANTRODOCO, a town of Naples, in Abruzzo Ultra 2da, in the district and 8 m. E of Citta-Ducale. Pop. 3,448.

ANTROS, an island on the W coast of France, at the mouth of the Gironde. The celebrated lighthouse called the Tour de Cordonan, is situated here, in N lat. 45° 35′ 11", W long. 1° 9′ 26". It is a revolving light, elevated 206 ft. above the sea.

ANTSIANACS, a tribe in Madagascar, occupying a district which extends from the sources of the Manangura to the country of the Antavarts on the E coast of the island. They have successfully resisted every endeavour on the part of Europeans to colonise their territory; yet they appear to be considerably advanced in civilization. They extract silver from mines in the mountains; and their land is productive and well-cultivated. The lake whence the Manangura issues is nearly 30 m. in circuit, and is said to be infested by crocodiles.
ANTSUKHI, a district of Caucasus, on the Samur. It is inhabited by 1,500 families of Mahommedan Lesghis, who have established a sort of independent republican government.

ANTUCO, a village and valley of Chili in the Cordilleras, watered by the river La Laja, which descends from a lake at the N and NE base of the volcanic mountain of A., and is one of the principal affluents of the Biobio. The valley—which extends to the very base of the volcano—is narrow, and affords few spots for cultivation, and is bounded on either side by precipices of appalling grandeur and height. The river sweeps along the base of the volcano over beds of scoriæ. An account of the ascent of the volcano from this valley will be found in the Athenœum for 1831. Professor Poeppig also ascended this mountain, which is situated in S lat. 36° 50', W long. 70° 30', and has an alt. of above 13,000 ft. He describes the summit as formed by "a small semicircular plain, from the middle of which rises a second but very depressed cone, which like a wall encircles the orifice, and consists of soft lava not above 50 ft. high." The circumf. of the crater he estimates at 600 paces. The summit is steep on all sides, but nearly perpendicular towards the N. After the peaks of ANT Teneriffe and Cotopaxi, the volcano of A. is probably the most pointed mountain known.
